title: Dog Hard Lump Under Skin Growing Rapidly: Causes and Next Steps
meta_desc: Discover what a rapidly growing hard lump on your dog might be, when to see a vet, and why photos are vital for triage. Urgency level: Medium.
content_html:
What Could a Rapidly Growing Hard Lump Be?

Finding a firm mass on your dog that appears to be increasing in size daily can be very stressful. There are several possibilities for such a growth, ranging from inflammatory responses like an abscess or an infected cyst to more concerning neoplastic growths such as mast cell tumors or soft tissue sarcomas. Because the texture is hard and the growth rate is aggressive, it is impossible to determine the cause through touch alone.

Is This an Emergency?

The urgency level for a rapidly growing hard lump is categorized as Medium. While it may not require an immediate trip to the ER tonight (unless it is bleeding profusely or your dog is having trouble breathing), you should schedule a veterinary appointment within the next 24 to 48 hours. Rapid growth is often a clinical sign of a high-grade tumor or an active infection, both of which require prompt medical intervention to prevent further complications.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

When you contact your veterinary clinic, providing a clear, well-lit photo of the lump is incredibly helpful. A photograph allows the triage team to see the exact location, the color of the overlying skin, and any signs of ulceration. If you place a common object like a coin next to the lump for scale, it provides a baseline measurement that helps the vet track exactly how fast the mass is expanding between the time you call and the time of your appointment.

faqs.question: Can a hard lump be a simple fatty tumor?
faqs.answer: Most fatty tumors, known as lipomas, are soft, movable, and grow very slowly. A hard, fast-growing lump is less likely to be a benign lipoma and should be evaluated by a professional.

faqs.question: What will the veterinarian do to diagnose the lump?
faqs.answer: The vet will likely perform a Fine Needle Aspirate (FNA), where a small needle collects cells from the mass to be examined under a microscope, or they may recommend a biopsy.

faqs.question: Should I try to squeeze or pop the lump?
faqs.answer: No, you should never attempt to squeeze or pop a hard lump. If it is a tumor, this can cause pain or trigger a dangerous inflammatory response; if it is an abscess, it could push the infection deeper into the body.
